literal: non-duality of the qualified; viśiṣṭa (qualified) + advaita (non-duality)
invariant_definition: Viśiṣṭādvaita is the school of Vedānta systematized by Rāmānuja (11th–12th c.), building on the Āḻvārs, Nāthamuni, and Yāmuna. It holds that reality is one (advaita), but that the one Brahman — identified with Nārāyaṇa / Viṣṇu — is qualified (viśiṣṭa) by an internal differentiation of sentient selves (cit) and insentient matter (acit), which stand to Brahman as body to embodied self (śarīra-śarīrin-bhāva). Difference is thus real and permanent, yet the souls and world are never independent: they are inseparable modes (prakāra) of the one Lord. Liberation is loving service and communion with Viṣṇu, attained through devotion (bhakti) and surrender (prapatti), not the erasure of individuality.
